بنیادی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بنیادی (buniyādī). By surface analysis, بنیاد (buniyād, “foundation, basis”) + ـی (-ī).

بنیان.e: Perhaps from बनिया (baniyā, “merchant”), characterizing their usual clothing.

senses.g: scented, aromatic; giving off a (either good or bad) smell or odor

ex.u: ہم بو دار باغ میں چلے گئے
ex.r: ham bū-dār gulšan mẽ cale ge
ex.e: we walked into a scented garden

senses.g: a title affixed in front female names

بوتل.e: Borrowed from Portuguese botelha. Compare Konkani बोत्ल (botla).

بوجھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑁄𑀚𑁆𑀛 (vojjha), from Sanskrit वह्य (vahyá), from Proto-Indo-European *wóǵʰ-yo-s (“fit to be carried”), from *weǵʰ- (“to carry, draw”).

senses.g: a shower, spray or burst of heavy wind, water or rain
Budapest (the capital and the most populous city of Hungary)

بوداپست.e: Borrowed from English Budapest, from Hungarian Budapest.

senses.g: Burewala (a city in Vehari district, Punjab, Pakistan)

بوڑھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀼𑀟𑁆𑀠 (vuḍḍha), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀯𑀼𑀠 (vuḍha /⁠vuḍḍha⁠/), from Sanskrit वृद्ध (vṛddhá).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀼𑀟𑁆𑀠 (vuḍḍha), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀯𑀼𑀠 (vuḍha /⁠vuḍḍha⁠/), from Sanskrit वृद्ध (vṛddhá).

senses.g: inflection of بُوڑھا (būṛhā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of بُوڑھا (būṛhā): — oblique masculine singular/plural
by means of, by way of

بوستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بوسْتَان (bōstān). By surface analysis, بو (bo, “fragrance”) + ـسْتان (-stān, “place of”).

senses.g: Bosnia (a geographic region of Bosnia and Herzegovina, consisting of the northern three fourths of the country)
Bosnia (a country in southeast Europe; in full, بوسنیا و ہرزیگووینا (bōsniyā va harzēgōvīnā))
speech, speaking, word, talk, conversation, delivery, utterance, cry, voice

بول.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀩𑁄𑀮𑁆𑀮 (*bolla) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix)

بولنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀩𑁄𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀇 (bŏllaï). Also compare بُلَانَا (bulānā, “to call”).

بوم.e: From Classical Persian بوم (būm, “earth, land”), from Middle Persian 𐭡𐭥𐭬 (būm), from Old Persian 𐏏 (būmiš), from Proto-Iranian *búHmiš, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰúHmiš.

بونا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀯𑀤𑀺 (vavadi), from Sanskrit वपति (vapati).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀫𑀡 (vāmaṇa), from Sanskrit वामन (vāmaná). Doublet of وامن (vāman), a tatsama.
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀫𑀡 (vāmaṇa), from Sanskrit वामन (vāmaná). Doublet of وامن (vāman), a tatsama.
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀫𑀡 (vāmaṇa), from Sanskrit वामन (vāmaná). Doublet of وامن (vāman), a tatsama.

بہادر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بهادر (bahādur). Compare Punjabi ਬਹਾਦਰ / بَہادُر (bahādur), Gujarati બહાદુર (bahādur), Marathi बहाद्दर (bahāddar), Bengali বাহাদুর (bahadur), Turkish bahadır.
Borrowed from Classical Persian بهادر (bahādur). Compare Punjabi ਬਹਾਦਰ / بَہادُر (bahādur), Gujarati બહાદુર (bahādur), Marathi बहाद्दर (bahāddar), Bengali বাহাদুর (bahadur), Turkish bahadır.

senses.g: prime of one's life; the bloom of youth

بہار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَهَار (bahār, “spring”). Doublet of وَسَنْت (vasant) and بَسَنْت (basant).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀳𑀸𑀭 (vihāra), from Sanskrit विहार (vihāra, “temple; monastery”).

بہاؤالدین.e: Borrowed from Arabic بَهَاءُ الدِّين (bahāʔu d-dīn, literally “beauty of faith”).

senses.g: Bahawalpur (a city in Bahawalpur district, Punjab, Pakistan)

بہاول پور.e: From بہاول (bahāval, “Nawab Mohammad Bahawal Khan II”) + پور (pūr).

senses.g: Bahawalnagar (a city in Bahawalnagar district, Punjab, Pakistan)

بہاء اللہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَهَاءُاللَّٰه (bahā'u-l-lāh), from Arabic بَهَاءُ ٱللّٰه (bahāʔu llāh, literally “Glory of God”).

ex.u: بس یہ بہت ہیں میرے لیے۔
ex.r: bas ye bahut ha͠i mere liye.
ex.e: That's it, this is enough for me.

بہت.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀳𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀢 (bahutta), from Sanskrit बहुत्व (bahutva, “muchness, abundance, multitude”), from Sanskrit बहु (bahu, “much, many, abundant”) from Proto-Indo-European *bʰenǵʰ- (“thick”). The development from a noun to an adjective was influenced by Prakrit 𑀅𑀳𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀢 (pahutta-), from Sanskrit प्रभूत (prabhūta, “come forth; abundant”). Compare Bengali বহুত (bohut), Gujarati બહુ (bahu) and Punjabi ਬਹੁਤ (bahut) / بَہُت (bahut).

بہتان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بُهْتَان (buhtān), borrowed from Arabic بُهْتَان (buhtān), from بَهَتَ (bahata).

senses.g: oblique plural of بُہْتَان (buhtān, “false accusation, slander, calumny”)

بہتر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بِهْتَر (bihtar). False cognate of English better.

بہر حال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بهر حال (ba-har hāl), from به (ba, “in”) + هر (har, “each”) + حال (hāl, “condition”).

senses.g: a male given name, Bahram, from Persian

بہرام.e: From Classical Persian بهرام (bahrām, “Bahram; Mars; 20ᵗʰ day of every solar month”).
From Classical Persian بهرام (bahrām, “Bahram; Mars; 20ᵗʰ day of every solar month”).

senses.g: a male given name, Behroz or Behroze, from Persian

ex.u:
سنتے ہیں جو بہشت کی تعریف سب درست
لیکن خدا کرے، وہ ترا جلوہ گاہ ہو
sunte ha͠i jo bahiśt kī tārīf sab durust
ex.r: lekin xudā kare voh tirā jalvā gāh ho
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

بہشت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بِهِشْت (bihišt), from Middle Persian 𐭥𐭧𐭱𐭲𐭩 (wahišt). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi بِہِشْت (bhśt). Compare Bengali বেহেশ্ত (beheśto). Doublet of بَسِیٹھ (basīṭh).

senses.g: Bahman, the eleventh month of the solar Persian calendar.

بہمن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بهمن (bahman), ultimately from Old Persian *Vahumanah (literally “good mind”).

senses.g: sister (a female sibling of any age)

بہن.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀪𑀇𑀡𑀺 (bhaïṇi), from Sanskrit भगिनी (bhaginī). Cognate with Punjabi بَھیْݨ (bhaiṇ).

بہنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀳𑀤𑀺 (vahadi), from Sanskrit वहति (váhati).

بہنوئی.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀩𑀳𑀺𑀡𑀯𑀇 (*bahiṇavaï), from Sanskrit भगिनीपति (bhaginīpati), from भगिनी (bhaginī, “sister”) + पति (páti, “husband”).

senses.g: vocative plural of بَہْنوئی (bahnoī, “brother-in-law; sister's husband”)
oblique plural of بَہْنوئی (bahnoī, “brother-in-law; sister's husband”)
bride; wife – the newly wedded wife in the family

بہو.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بهو (bhv /⁠bahū⁠/) (c. 1609),, from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀳𑀽 (vahū), 𑀯𑀥𑀽 (vadhū), from Sanskrit वधू (vadhū́).

senses.g: mild whitening disease in the pupil

بیان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَیَان (bayān), borrowed from Arabic بَيَان (bayān).

بیاہ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀯𑀸𑀳 (vivāha), from Sanskrit वि॒वा॒ह (vivāhá). Doublet of وِواہ (vivāh), بِواہ (bivāh), and بِباہ (bibāh)

بیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیت (bayt), from Arabic بَيْت (bayt).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بیت (bayt), from Arabic بَيْت (bayt).

senses.g: Bethlehem (a city in the West Bank, Palestine)

بیت اللحم.e: From Arabic بَيْت لَحْم (bayt laḥm), ultimately from Hebrew בֵּית לֶחֶם (bet lékhem).

senses.g: Jerusalem (a city in Israel; a city in Palestine)

بیت المقدس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَیْتُالْمُقَدَّس (baytu-l-muqaddas), from Arabic بَيْت الْمَقْدِس (bayt al-maqdis).

senses.g: Bethany (a village in ancient Israel, in the modern West Bank)

بیت عنیاہ.e: From Aramaic בית עניא (“house of suffering”).

senses.g: alternative form of بَیت اَلْلَحَم (bait allaham)

بیٹا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بیٿا (byṭa), from Prakrit 𑀩𑀺𑀝𑁆𑀝 (biṭṭa)., further etymology unknown. Perhaps inherited from Sanskrit बटु (baṭu, “boy, lad, chap”).

senses.g: inflection of بیٹا (beṭā, “boy; son; child”): — direct plural
inflection of بیٹا (beṭā, “boy; son; child”): — oblique/vocative singular

بیٹھنا.e: Derived from Prakrit 𑀉𑀯𑀯𑀺𑀝𑁆𑀞 (uvaviṭṭha), from Sanskrit उपविष्ट (upaviṣṭa), from उप- (upa-) + वि॒ष्ट (viṣṭá).

بیج.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit बीज (bī́ja). Compare Hindi बीआ (bīā), a tadbhava.

senses.g: out of place, ill-placed, misplaced, ill-timed
Beijing (a direct-administered municipality, the capital city of China)

ex.e: Put all the stuff in the middle.

بیچ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘 (vicca), from Sanskrit वीच्य (vīcya). Cognate Punjabi وِچّ (vicc).

ex.u: سارا سامان اُس کے بیچ ڈال دو۔
ex.r: sārā sāmān us ke bīc ḍāl do.
ex.e: Put all the stuff in their midst.

بیچ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀘 (vicca), from Sanskrit वीच्य (vīcya). Cognate Punjabi وِچّ (vicc).

بیچنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑁂𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀤𑀺 (veccadi), from Sanskrit *वेत्ययति (*vetyayati), which was denominalised from one of the derivatives of the root वी (vī); compare Sanskrit वेति (véti, “to procure”), वेतन (vetana, “wages; price”).

بیخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیخ (bēx, “root; base”). The pronunciation with ī is via Arabic.

senses.g: selflessness (More than mere selflessness, it describes a state of ecstasy and joyous intoxication resulting from a state of unity with God.)

بیدق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَیدَق (baydaq), from Arabic بَيْدَق (baydaq), back-formation from بَيَادِق (bayādiq), from Middle Persian 𐭯𐭣𐭠𐭲𐭪' (pdʾtk' /⁠payādag, payādak⁠/). Doublet of پِیادَہ (piyāda).

بیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیر (bīr), from Middle Persian wyl (wīr), from Proto-Indo-European *wiHrós. Cognate with Avestan 𐬬𐬍𐬭𐬀 (vīra), Sanskrit वीर (vīra, “man (especially a brave one), chief, husband”), Northern Kurdish wêrek (“courageous”), Kashmiri ویٖر (vīr, “hero”), Ossetian ир (ir), Latin vir (“man”) Lithuanian výras, and English wer.

Borrowed from Classical Persian بیر (bīr), from Middle Persian wyl (wīr), from Proto-Indo-European *wiHrós. Cognate with Avestan 𐬬𐬍𐬭𐬀 (vīra), Sanskrit वीर (vīra, “man (especially a brave one), chief, husband”), Northern Kurdish wêrek (“courageous”), Kashmiri ویٖر (vīr, “hero”), Ossetian ир (ir), Latin vir (“man”) Lithuanian výras, and English wer.

senses.g: alternative spelling of بے روزگاری (“unemployment; joblessness”)
Beed (a city in central Maharashtra, India)

ex.u: چین کا بیڑا اس وقت بحر الکاہل میں ہے۔
ex.r: cīn kā beṛā is vaqt bahar-ul-kāhil mẽ hai.
ex.e: China's fleet is currently in the Pacific Ocean.

بیڑا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑁂𑀟𑀕 (beḍaga), from Sanskrit बेडक (beḍaka), बेडा (beḍā). Cognate to Sindhi ٻِيڙِي (ḇīṛī).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑁂𑀠 (veḍha), from Sanskrit वेष्ट (veṣṭa). Doublet of بیڑھ (beṛh), بیڑ (beṛ), and بیڑِی (beṛī).

senses.g: to be destroyed, devastated, lose everything

ex.u: پورے ملک کا بیڑا غرق ہوگیا۔
ex.r: pūre mulk kā beṛā ġarq ho gayā.
ex.e: The whole country has gone down the drain.

بیس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀻𑀲 (vīsa), from Sanskrit विं॒श॒ति (viṃśatí).

بیستی.e: Corrupted from بے عزتی (be ’izzatī, “insult, disrespect”). Cognate with Punjabi بیسْتی (bestī) / بِسْتی (bistī).

ex.r: be śak yih merī qamīz hai.
ex.e: For sure, this is my shirt.

ex.u: آپ بیشک اس طرف سے چلے جائیں۔
ex.r: āp be śak is taraf se cale jāẽ.
ex.e: You can go this way if you like.

بیشک.e: From بے (be, “without”) + شک (śak, “doubt”). Compare Persian بلا شک (belâ šak, “undoubtedly”).

بیضا.e: Borrowed from Arabic بَيْضَاء (bayḍāʔ), feminine singular of أَبْيَض (ʔabyaḍ).
Borrowed from Arabic بَيْضَاء (bayḍāʔ), feminine singular of أَبْيَض (ʔabyaḍ).

بیضہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیضه (bayza), from Arabic بَيْضَة (bayḍa).

senses.g: act of swearing allegiance; fealty (especially religiously)

بیعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بَیعَت (bay'at), borrowed from Arabic بَيْعَة (bayʕa). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi بیعت (by't /⁠bai'at⁠/).

بیگ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیگ (bēg), itself borrowed from Proto-Turkic *bēg via a Turkic language.

بیگار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیگار (bē-gār, “forced labour”). By surface analysis, بے (be, “without”) + گار (gār, “doer”).

بیگانہ.e: Inherited from Old Hindi बेगांनां (begā̃nā̃), from Classical Persian بیگانه (bēgāna). Compare Punjabi ਬਿਗਾਨਾ (bigānā) / بِگانہ (bigānā).

بیگم.e: From Classical Persian بیگم (bēgum). Ultimately from Proto-Turkic.

بیل.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑁂𑀮𑁆𑀮𑀻 (vĕllī), from Sanskrit वेल्लि (velli). Cognate with Gujarati વેલ (vel), Marathi वेल (vel), Punjabi ویل (vel) / ਵੇਲ (vel).

senses.g: Belgium (a country in Western Europe that has borders with the Netherlands, Germany, Luxembourg and France)

بیلچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیلْچَه (bēlča), diminutive of بیل (bēl), from Middle Persian [script needed] (byl /⁠bēl⁠/).

بیلن.e: Inherited from Sanskrit वेल्लन (vellana, “shaking; rolling pin”). Cognate with Bengali বেলন (belon), Gujarati વેલણ (velaṇ), Punjabi ویلْݨا (velṇā).

senses.g: Bela (a city in Lasbela district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

بیماری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیمَارِی (bēmārī). By surface analysis, بیمار (bīmār) + ی (ī).

بین.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀯𑀬𑀡 (vayaṇa) Sanskrit वचन (vacana).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بین (bayn), from Arabic بَيْن (bayn).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بین (bayn), from Arabic بَيْن (bayn).
Borrowed from Classical Persian بین (bayn), from Arabic بَيْن (bayn).

senses.g: pungi (a wind instrument used by snake charmers)

بین الاقوامی.e: Coined in Urdu from Arabic بَيْنَ الأَقْوَام (bayna l-ʔaqwām, “between the peoples”) + ـی (-ī), by analogy with Classical Persian بینالمللی (baynalmilalī). The Arabic word قَوْم (qawm) is used (loaned via Classical Persian) in the sense of "nation" as is standard in Urdu. In Arabic itself, the word دُوَلِيّ (duwaliyy) has become standardised for "international". Also compare Ottoman Turkish بینالملل (beyn-elmilel)

بین السطور.e: Borrowed from Arabic بَيْنَ ٱلسُّطُور (bayna s-suṭūr, literally “between the lines”).
Borrowed from Arabic بَيْنَ ٱلسُّطُور (bayna s-suṭūr, literally “between the lines”).
From Arabic بَيْن (bayn, “between, separation”) + Arabic السُّطُور (as-suṭūr, “lines”).
From Arabic بَيْن (bayn, “between, separation”) + Arabic السُّطُور (as-suṭūr, “lines”).

بین الکلیات.e: Borrowed from Arabic بَيْنَ ٱلكُلِّيَات (bayna l-kulliyāt, literally “between the colleges”). The Arabic word كُلِّيَّة (kulliyya) loaned (via Persian) as کُلِّیَہ (kulliya) in Urdu, however, is not used for "college"; کالِج (kālij), derived from the English word, is used instead.

بین الملکی.e: From Arabic بَيْن (bayn, “between, separation”) + Arabic المُلْكِيّ (al-mulkiyy, “relating to sovereignty or dominion”). The Arabic word مُلْك (mulk) (loaned via Classical Persian) is used in the dated sense of "land, country" as is standard in Urdu.

بین الملی.e: From Arabic بَيْن (bayn, “between, separation”) + Arabic المِلِّيّ (al-milliyy, “pertaining to a religious community”). The Arabic word مِلَّة (milla) (loaned via Classical Persian) is used in the dated sense of "nation, people" as is current in Persian and Urdu مِلَّت (millat).

بین النجوم.e: Borrowed from Arabic بَيْنَ ٱلنُجُوم (bayna n-nujūm, literally “between the stars”).

بینگن.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀯𑀸𑀇𑀁𑀕𑀡 (vāiṃgaṇa), from Sanskrit वातिङ्गण (vātiṅgaṇa), from Proto-Dravidian *waẓVtV. Doublet of بادنجان (bādanjān).

بیوقوف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیوقوف (bēwuqūf), from بیـ (bē-, “without”) + Arabic وُقُوف (wuqūf, “standing still; study, knowledge”). Compare Bengali বেকুব (bekub), Gujarati બેવકૂફ (bevakūph).

بیوہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیوَه (bēwa). Doublet of وِدَھوا (vidhavā) and بِدھوا (bidhvā).

senses.g: not, non, un-, in-, dis-, ir, -less, im-
the name of the second letter of the Perso-Arabic alphabet, ب (b).
without control or authority, helplessness, powerless

بے اختیار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیاختیار. By surface analysis, بے (be) + اختیار.
Borrowed from Classical Persian بیاختیار. By surface analysis, بے (be) + اختیار.

بے انتہا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بی اِنْتِهَا (bē intihā). By surface analysis, بے (be, “without”) + اِنْتِہا (intihā, “limit”).

senses.g: same definitions as adjective; see above
a sea of people; many people.

بے انتہا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بی اِنْتِهَا (bē intihā). By surface analysis, بے (be, “without”) + اِنْتِہا (intihā, “limit”).

بے ایمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیایمان (bē-īmān). By surface analysis, بے (be) + ایمان (īmān, “faith”).

بے تحاشا.e: Derived from Classical Persian تَحَاشَى (tahāšā), from Arabic تَحَاشَى (taḥāšā, “to avoid”), with the prefix بے (be).

بے ترتیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیترتیب (betartīb). By surface analysis, بے (be, “not”) + ترتیب (tartīb, “order”).

بے حس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بی حس.

بے خبری.e: From بے خبر (be xabar, “unaware”) + ـی (-ī, “act of”).

بے دانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیدانه. By surface analysis, بے (be) + دانہ.

ex.u: بے شَک خُدا سَب جانْنے والا ہے۔
ex.r: be śak xudā sab jānne vālā he.

ex.u: آپ بے شک اس طرف سے چلے جائیں۔
ex.r: āp be śak is taraf se cale jāẽ.
ex.e: You can go this way if you like.

بے شک.e: From بے (be, “without”) + شک (śak, “doubt”). Compare Persian بلا شک (belâ šak, “undoubtedly”).

بے عزتی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بی عِزَّتِی (bē izzatī), from بی (bē) + عِزَّت (izzat) + ـِی (-ī). By surface analysis, بے عِزَّت (be 'izzat, “without respect”) + ـِی (-ī, “act of”).

بے غرض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیغرض. By surface analysis, بے (be) + غرض.

بے غور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بیغور (bē-ġōr). By surface analysis, بے (be) + غور (ġor).

بے مثال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بی مثال (be-misāl). By surface analysis, بے (be) + مِثال (misāl).

senses.g: benami, offshore, undisclosed (specifically declared in someone else's name)

ex.u:
2019, ریاض سہیل, بےنامی کھاتے اور اکاؤنٹ ہوتے کیا ہیں؟, BBC Urdu
وزیراعظم عمران خان نے پیر کو قوم کے نام ایک پیغام میں کہا کہ تمام ایسے افراد جو بےنامی بینک اکاؤنٹ، بے نامی اثاثے اور ملک سے باہر اثاثے رکھتے ہیں وہ 30 جون تک ایمنسٹی سکیم سے فائدہ اٹھا کر اپنے اثاثے ظاہر کر دیں۔
ex.e: On Monday, Prime Minister Imran Khan had nationally stated that individuals with undeclared bank accounts and undeclared assets and offshore assets can make use of the Amnesty scheme to declare their assets by 30th June.

بے نامی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian بی نامی (bē-nāmī, “nameless”). Equivalent to بے (be, “without”) + نامی (nāmī, “(pertaining to a) name”).

بھابی.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بھابی (bhaby /⁠bhābī⁠/) (c. 1697) and Middle Hindi بھابھی (bhabhy /⁠bhābhī⁠/), from Prakrit *𑀪𑀸𑀩𑁆𑀪 (*bhābbha), from baby talk. Cognate with Punjabi بھابی (bhābī).

senses.g: direct plural of بھابی (bhābī, “sister-in-law (brother's (or friend's) wife)”)
vocative plural of بھابی (bhābī, “sister-in-law (brother's (or friend's) wife)”)
oblique plural of بھابی (bhābī, “sister-in-law (brother's (or friend's) wife)”)
alternative form of بھابی (bhābī, “sister-in-law (brother's (or friend's) wife)”)

بھاپ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بھاپھہ (bhaphh /⁠bhāph⁠/) (c. 1419) and later as بھاپ (bhap /⁠bhāp⁠/), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀩𑀧𑁆𑀨 (bappha), from Sanskrit वाष्प (vāṣpa, “tear”).

senses.g: Bhadrapada, the sixth month of the Hindi lunar calendar

بھادوں.e: Inherited from Prakrit *भद्दवअ (bhaddavaa), from Sanskrit भाद्रपद (bhādrapada). Cognate with Punjabi ਭਾਦੋਂ (bhādõ).

senses.g: India, Bharat (a country in South Asia)
go to hell!; go away!; burn in hell!

ex.u: اَپْنا یِہ بھاشَن کِسی اَور کو دینا۔
ex.r: apnā ye bhāśan kisī aur ko denā.
ex.e: Give this lecture to someone else.

senses.g: Bhag (a city in Kachhi district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

بھاگنا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀪𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀢𑀺 (*bhaggati), a verbal derivation from Sanskrit भग्न (bhagna, “broken, defeated, lost”), from the root भञ्ज् (bhañj). Cognate with Nepali भाग्नु (bhāgnu), Gujarati ભાગવું (bhāgvũ), as well as Punjabi ਭੱਜਣਾ (bhajjaṇā) from a different derivation.

بھانجا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi بھانجا (bhanja), from Prakrit 𑀪𑀸𑀬𑀡𑀺𑀚𑁆𑀚 (bhāyaṇijja) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit भागिनेय (bhāginēya).

senses.g: vocative plural of بھان٘جا (bhāñjā, “sororal nephew (sister's son)”)
oblique plural of بھان٘جا (bhāñjā, “sororal nephew (sister's son)”)

بھانجی.e: Feminine of بھان٘جا (bhāñjā, “nephew”), from Prakrit 𑀪𑀸𑀬𑀡𑀺𑀚𑁆𑀚 (bhāyaṇijja) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit भागिनेय (bhāginēya).

senses.g: direct plural of بھان٘جی (bhāñjī, “sororal niece (sister's daughter)”)
vocative plural of بھان٘جی (bhāñjī, “sororal niece (sister's daughter)”)
oblique plural of بھان٘جی (bhāñjī, “sororal niece (sister's daughter)”)
inflection of بھان٘جا (bhāñjā, “sororal nephew (sister's son)”): — direct plural
inflection of بھان٘جا (bhāñjā, “sororal nephew (sister's son)”): — oblique/vocative singular

بھانگ.e: Inherited from Sanskrit भङ्ग (bhaṅga). Doublet of بَنْگ (baṅg) via Persian.

senses.g: Bhavnagar (a city in Saurashtra, Gujarat, India)

بھاؤ نگر.e: Borrowed from Gujarati ભાવનગર (bhāvnagar), named after Bhavsinhji I Ratanji.

senses.g: brother (a male sibling of any age)

بھائی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀪𑀸𑀇 (bhāi), 𑀪𑀸𑀉 (bhāu), 𑀪𑀸𑀆 (bhāā), from Sanskrit भ्रातृ (bhrā́tṛ), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *bʰráHtā, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *bʰráHtā, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰréh₂tēr (“brother”).

بھتیجا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi भतीजा (bhatījā), from Prakrit 𑀪𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀚𑁆𑀚 (bhattijja), from Sanskrit भ्रा॒त्रीय॒ (bhrātrī́ya).
